channel_name: Invest Like the Best · Patrick O'Shaughnessy
video_title: Legendary Investor Dan Loeb on AI, Credit, & Third Point’s $25B Strategy
gist: Dan Loeb (Third Point, ~$25B) lays out a bullish, non-bubble thesis on AI/semis as his single largest sector exposure (Nvidia at ~12-15x forward, plus semi-cap/hyperscalers), explains how Third Point's cross-capital-structure 'fulcrum security' lens let it make Twitter and XAI its largest credit positions, and argues credit, human-behavior anomalies, and corporate transactions remain durable alpha sources even as AI commoditizes pure data work.

themes.theme: AI/semis positioning — the biggest book is tech

insights.claim: Third Point's largest sector exposure and the bulk of its hedge-fund capital is in AI semis, semi-cap equipment and hyperscalers, which Loeb calls the single most attractive sector in the market right now.
insights.speaker: Dan Loeb
insights.so_what: A value-rooted, multi-strategy CIO is overweight AI infrastructure rather than taking profits; signals continued institutional support for the trade.
insights.quote: Yeah, I think it's the most attractive sector right now. It's where the bulk of our capital is invested.

insights.claim: Loeb argues Nvidia is a catch-up trade at roughly 12-15x forward earnings (FY27/FY28) for one of the fastest-growing companies of its size, and chose not to take profits across his semis/hyperscaler book after reviewing valuations vs growth rates.
insights.speaker: Dan Loeb
insights.so_what: Frames mega-cap AI leaders as reasonably priced on forward earnings, not bubble-valued; a low-teens multiple on a fast grower is the explicit bull case.
insights.quote: there's such a catch-up trade in Nvidia at 15 times 27 12* 28 for the most dominant fastest... very fast growing company at its size

insights.claim: Loeb explicitly rejects the AI-bubble comparison to the dot-com era: today's leaders fund capex off their own balance sheets and generate enormous cash, with no valuation bubble like 2000, so a bear case requires believing the capex earns no return.
insights.speaker: Dan Loeb
insights.so_what: Counters the loudest bear thesis; the tell to watch is post-capex free cash flow and whether AI capex actually yields returns.
insights.quote: it's very different from the dot bubble which we were short... you don't have the valuation bubble now on those companies that you had on... those companies back in those days

insights.claim: The SOX semiconductor index is up ~40% this year, an event Loeb says he's never seen, driven by fundamentals in semis/semi-cap/memory being far stronger than the already-high expectations.
insights.speaker: Dan Loeb
insights.so_what: Quantifies the magnitude of the move and grounds it in earnings, not just multiple expansion.
insights.quote: the socks is up 40%. I... don't think I've ever seen an event like that

insights.claim: Loeb dates the semiconductor regime change to Nvidia's blowout March quarter ~3 years ago, after which semis went from being 'roadkill'/'left for dead' to the market's central trade, and says latecomers could still play catch-up.
insights.speaker: Dan Loeb
insights.so_what: Frames the AI trade as still investable years in; pattern argues against assuming you've 'missed it.'
insights.quote: semiconductors were kind of left for dead. They were like roadkill in the market... that all changed when Nvidia reported its March results three years ago

insights.claim: Loeb uses Jensen Huang's AI 'stack' (power/energy at the base, then chips/infrastructure, then LLMs/software/applications) as his core mental model and says Third Point has played it via industrials, infrastructure and hyperscalers.
insights.speaker: Dan Loeb
insights.so_what: A reusable map for expressing AI exposure across the whole value chain, including unglamorous power/industrials at the base.
insights.quote: starting with uh power and energy at the bottom and... chips and infrastructure and moving up through LMS and software and applications

insights.claim: Loeb tracks competing AI compute ecosystems — Nvidia vs Trainium vs TPU — and their relative strength as the key driver of how value flows through the different hyperscalers.
insights.speaker: Dan Loeb
insights.so_what: Custom-silicon adoption (Trainium/TPU) vs merchant GPUs is the swing factor for hyperscaler and Nvidia relative performance.
insights.quote: thinking about... the Nvidia the tranium and the TPU ecosystems and how those play out and relative strength and how that plays through the different hyperscalers

insights.claim: Loeb names Nvidia, Anthropic, and 'Elon World' (Elon Musk's companies collectively) as the three most consequential companies today and uses them as his prism for understanding how AI flows through markets.
insights.speaker: Dan Loeb
insights.so_what: Identifies which private/public entities he watches as leading indicators for the whole complex.
insights.quote: the probably the three most consequential companies today. Nvidia, Anthropic, and Elon World, you know, all of his companies collectively

insights.claim: Loeb cites Anthropic's revenue growth, product adoption and next-generation models as evidence that enterprise AI is 'barely scratching the surface,' placing him firmly in the AI optimist camp.
insights.speaker: Dan Loeb
insights.so_what: Bullish read on AI application-layer monetization runway; supports continued enterprise AI spend.
insights.quote: You look at Anthropic's revenue growth and the adoption and the usefulness of its products... We're... barely scratching the surface

insights.claim: Loeb argues you can no longer 'punt on tech' and focus only on industrials/consumer/healthcare; being a tech investor is now mandatory because tech is a big, compounding share of the economy that affects everything else.
insights.speaker: Dan Loeb
insights.so_what: A self-described non-native-tech, value-rooted manager declaring tech literacy non-optional — a signal for allocator due diligence.
insights.quote: I think you have to be a tech person today. It's such a... big and growing and compounding part of the economy. It affects everything else.

themes.theme: Macro lens — oil/geopolitics and AI dominate

insights.claim: Loeb says the conventional macro variables (growth, unemployment, inflation, rates, currencies, gold, crypto) are currently trumped by just two factors: oil (driven by war/geopolitics) and AI (spending/infrastructure plus its societal/economic impact).
insights.speaker: Dan Loeb
insights.so_what: Tells you which macro inputs a top CIO is actually weighting; oil and AI capex are the dashboard, not the usual prints.
insights.quote: all that stuff is trumped right now by two things... Where's oil and that's going to be dictated by what happens in the war and geopolitics and what's happening with AI

insights.claim: Loeb is tactical over strategic and deliberately avoids minute-to-minute news, focusing only on what's relevant to the economy and his positions to avoid being driven crazy by noise.
insights.speaker: Dan Loeb
insights.so_what: Process insight: filter the firehose to position-relevant signal; an 'essentialism' discipline applied to information.
insights.quote: I try not to get too obsessed with the minute-to-minute stuff because that will drive you crazy. I try to be a little more tactical than strategic.

insights.claim: Loeb invokes Eric Schmidt's ~2013 Davos warning that accelerating technological disruption is not an anomaly, arguing innovation has since 'logarithmically accelerated' and investors must adapt continuously rather than expect mean-reversion to steady growth.
insights.speaker: Dan Loeb
insights.so_what: Frames pace of change as accelerating, not reverting — argues for adaptive strategy and against static value frameworks.
insights.quote: Hold on to your seats... things are only going to accelerate from here. And he was really right.

insights.claim: Third Point made the Twitter financing debt its largest credit position at the time, buying at ~96-97 cents on the dollar for a ~12% yield when most credit investors were too scared to touch it, after Morgan Stanley resold the deeply underwater Musk-acquisition debt near par.
insights.speaker: Dan Loeb
insights.so_what: Concrete contrarian credit trade: buying hated, mispriced acquisition debt at a fat yield when underlying business value supports it.
insights.quote: Most credit investors were really scared and nervous to buy that even though it was like 96 97 cents on the dollar. It was yielding around a 12% yield.

insights.claim: Third Point bought XAI's debt financing when almost no credit investors would, despite no cash flow, ~$2B revenue and a ~$20B enterprise value, because its equity/private-investing knowledge gave it conviction XAI was a real business.
insights.speaker: Dan Loeb
insights.so_what: Demonstrates lending into pre-cash-flow AI businesses using equity-side conviction — a template for AI-infrastructure credit underwriting.
insights.quote: there was no cash flow... $2 billion in revenues and a $20 billion uh enterprise value, but we were very comfortable that this was a real business

insights.claim: Loeb's central edge is identifying the 'fulcrum security' — the part of the capital structure with the best risk/reward — using a comprehensive view across debt and equity; in the Credit Suisse/UBS deal the holdco paper had the most upside while the prefs (AT1) were wiped out.
insights.speaker: Dan Loeb
insights.so_what: Reusable restructuring framework; in stressed deals, placement in the cap structure (holdco vs opco vs pref) determines outcome more than the directional call.
insights.quote: The fulcrum there was actually the hold coat paper that had the most upside but the opco paper... also did well. The pref was wiped out.

insights.claim: Loeb personally 'supersizes' specific opportunities like Twitter and XAI even though he is only the hedge-fund PM and isn't on the investment committees of Third Point's private-credit, CLO, structured-credit or high-yield businesses, each of which has its own PM.
insights.speaker: Dan Loeb
insights.so_what: Clarifies governance/structure of the $25B platform; CIO intervention is selective and opportunity-driven, not blanket.
insights.quote: I will come in if there's like an interesting opportunity like we superersize both of those both Twitter and XAI when I got involved

themes.theme: Third Point's evolution — from event-driven value to quality + credit platform

insights.claim: Third Point's roots are in event-driven/distressed credit; Loeb's early framework (from Joel Greenblatt's 'You Can Be a Stock Market Genius') exploited cheap newly created securities — spin-offs, demutualizations, privatizations, post-reorg equities — sold indiscriminately by holders, with managers who sandbagged numbers at the time their incentives were set.
insights.speaker: Dan Loeb
insights.so_what: The structural-mispricing playbook (liquidity gaps + sandbagged guidance + underoptimized subsidiaries) that Loeb says still works today.
insights.quote: a new security is often created which is priced and valued at a very cheap price because of a lack of liquidity

insights.claim: Loeb says investors who underperformed or didn't survive the last decade were the ones stubbornly stuck on deep-value low multiples and unwilling to move into higher-multiple, faster-growing, higher-ROIC 'quality' businesses.
insights.speaker: Dan Loeb
insights.so_what: A pointed warning that rigid low-multiple value was the losing posture; adaptability into quality/growth is framed as survival.
insights.quote: people who have underperformed or haven't survived the last decade... were really stuck on the idea of... deep value low multiples and being really stubborn

insights.claim: Loeb says the best opportunity today overlays the old event-driven mispricing lens with a quality/business-quality lens, citing 'The Outsiders' (capital allocators like Danaher, TransDigm) and Cunningham's 'Quality Investing' as the most influential books on his evolution.
insights.speaker: Dan Loeb
insights.so_what: Combining structural cheapness with durable moats/high ROIC is his stated current edge; a reading list for the approach.

insights.claim: Loeb warns that last year was the worst for ostensibly high-quality compounders because AI disruption rapidly made many 'apparently high-quality' businesses less so — a regime shift that broke the buy-and-hold quality playbook.
insights.speaker: Dan Loeb
insights.so_what: Even moaty compounders carry AI-obsolescence risk now; quality must be re-underwritten against disruption, not assumed durable.
insights.quote: a lot of these companies that appeared to be super high quality, it's probably the worst year... because the disruption of AI, a lot of these apparently highquality companies um very rapidly became less

insights.claim: Third Point is a multi-business platform of ~$25B: the flagship hedge fund grew from $3M to ~$9B (now ~30% credit), plus a ~$7B CLO business, ~$3B (≈30%) in structured/corporate credit, ~$1B insurance credit, a couple billion in asbestos liabilities, a small new private-credit unit, and a venture-capital business.
insights.speaker: Dan Loeb
insights.so_what: Maps the actual AUM composition; ~60% credit overall makes Third Point as much a credit shop as an equity hedge fund.
insights.quote: which started at three million. It's now about $9 billion today... we have um a CLO business with about $7 billion in it

insights.claim: Loeb dialed back hedge-fund risk going into 'the war' and for the first time since 2009 ran more credit than equity exposure, before quickly rotating risk back up to a more-equity-than-credit stance.
insights.speaker: Dan Loeb
insights.so_what: Shows real-time defensive rotation into credit at stress and back into equity — the optionality of a multi-asset book in practice.
insights.quote: for the first time since 2009 we did have more credit exposure than equity but we very quickly have taken that back up

insights.claim: Loeb describes the hedge fund's typical equity posture as roughly 110% long / 30-40% short, though it moves around substantially with the risk environment.
insights.speaker: Dan Loeb
insights.so_what: Concrete gross/net framing of the flagship's equity book for sizing the directional bet behind his AI bullishness.
insights.quote: our equity book is generically around 110 long by 30 or 40 short but that can be all over the place

themes.theme: Where alpha survives AI — human behavior, credit, deals

insights.claim: Loeb argues the durable edge for fundamental investors is exploiting forced behavior by quants, CTAs and pod shops whose risk metrics force selling into declines — irrational for long-term holders but baked into those models — creating persistent anomalies.
insights.speaker: Dan Loeb
insights.so_what: Structural-flow alpha: fade forced de-grossing/stop-outs; the proliferation of risk-managed strategies manufactures the dislocations to trade against.
insights.quote: They have... risk metrics which have forced selling on the way down. And so they do the opposite of what it might be rational

insights.claim: Loeb cites repeated 'good-numbers, falling-stock' episodes (Nvidia after a monster quarter, Micron up ~80% revenue, Meta years ago) as proof that expectations getting too high causes sell-offs on strong prints — the 'Wile E. Coyote' moment when there's no marginal buyer left.
insights.speaker: Dan Loeb
insights.so_what: Trade the expectations gap, not the fundamentals; positioning/exhaustion of buyers can tank a stock on great results.
insights.quote: stock went up was like wy coyote. There was no one else to buy the stock. get tanked

insights.claim: Loeb predicts AI will not eliminate the capital allocator's job for the next couple of years because companies still need to raise money, securities must change hands, and a human interface is needed; a fully AI-managed capital system is highly unlikely.
insights.speaker: Dan Loeb
insights.so_what: Tempers AI-replaces-investors fears; near-term, intermediation and human judgment persist as moats.
insights.quote: I think it's highly unlikely that there will be a completely... AI managed capital system

insights.claim: Loeb argues AI cannot do private equity, creditors'-committee work, restructurings or high-touch private credit because deals require human negotiation — so the private and stressed-credit end of the continuum is structurally protected from automation.
insights.speaker: Dan Loeb
insights.so_what: Reinforces why Loeb leans into credit/private markets as AI-proof alpha; the harder-to-automate the process, the more durable the edge.
insights.quote: It's hard to imagine the computers sitting on a creditor's committee and working through the capital structure and being able to transact.

insights.claim: Loeb invokes 'there's nothing new under the sun' (Reminiscences of a Stock Operator / Ecclesiastes): hysteria, bubbles, panics and the extremes of human nature won't be removed by AI, and may even get re-encoded under labels like 'risk management.'
insights.speaker: Dan Loeb
insights.so_what: Behavioral cycles remain the bedrock alpha source even in an AI-driven market; AI may amplify, not dampen, herding.
insights.quote: the thing that... doesn't change is... hysteras... bubbles... panics and just the extremes of human nature both optimistically and pessimistically

insights.claim: Loeb insists boards' primary duty is shareholder value and criticizes the Business Roundtable's stakeholder pivot as a distraction; he says good treatment of communities/employees/products is part of — not in conflict with — creating shareholder value.
insights.speaker: Dan Loeb
insights.so_what: A clear shareholder-primacy stance from an activist; signals which boards he'd view as drifting from fiduciary duty.
insights.quote: the business roundt said we're no longer going to... say that... the board's responsibility is primarily to drive shareholder value... I think it was a distraction

insights.claim: Loeb says the main source of bad governance is boards letting loyalty to an underperforming CEO override their duty to shareholders, since boards set strategy and accountability but don't run companies.
insights.speaker: Dan Loeb
insights.so_what: Defines the activist trigger: CEO-board capture; watch for boards too deferential to weak management.
insights.quote: they let their loyalty or relationship to a CEO who's not up to the job overshadow their duty to shareholders. That's probably the main thing

insights.claim: Loeb identifies social pressure via writing and PR as activism's most effective lever — alongside financial (bids) and legal (proxy contests, litigation, information requests) levers — because public letters shake up boards and rally other shareholders.
insights.speaker: Dan Loeb
insights.so_what: Explains the strategic role of Loeb's famous letters; reputational pressure can move outcomes without a proxy fight.
insights.quote: Social pressure is actually a very effective way and I think the best way to... put social pressure on a company is through writing and PR efforts

insights.claim: Loeb confirms his activism often targets high-status companies/CEOs not earning that status; in Sotheby's ('Sabes') Third Point bought 9.9%, replaced a CEO lacking art/collector depth with MSG's Tad Smith, cleaned up operations, and the company was ultimately sold.
insights.speaker: Dan Loeb
insights.so_what: Illustrates the 'status gap' screen and the operational/CEO-change activist playbook that produced a realized exit.
insights.quote: We brought in guy named Tad Smith who was terrific from MSG... cleaned up the operations, improved the technology and then... they sold the company.

insights.claim: Loeb says classic 'fix the bad management' activism mostly survives in the sub-$2B market cap space, but Third Point now prefers backing already-great management over turnarounds, because badly-run companies tend to have ten times more hidden problems ('a morass').
insights.speaker: Dan Loeb
insights.so_what: Signals a strategic shift away from deep turnaround activism toward 'quality + cheering on good management'; small-cap mispricing is where pure activism remains.
insights.quote: we'd much rather invest in... a great company with awesome management... than... find something that but for... mismanagement the company would be worth a lot more

insights.claim: Loeb is more bullish on Korea, Taiwan and Japan as hunting grounds for great companies, sees Israel as a niche market with a top-performing portfolio holding despite the war, and views European local-economy-dependent businesses as challenged by regulation and an anti-capitalist attitude.
insights.speaker: Dan Loeb
insights.so_what: Geographic tilt: Asia ex-China and select Israel over domestic-Europe; he still owns export/quality European names like Rolls-Royce and ASML.
insights.quote: there's a lot going on in Korea, Taiwan, Japan. I'm probably more bullish on them... The European markets are... just tough right now given the regulatory environment

insights.claim: Loeb says Japanese activism is hard and slow — Third Point's Sony stake (once ~7%) took ~5 years to bear fruit — but the government wants reform while entrenched management resists; Japan is now breaking up cross-shareholdings and penalizing companies trading below book value.
insights.speaker: Dan Loeb
insights.so_what: Japan reform (ROIC focus, below-book penalties, unwinding cross-holdings) is a real, government-backed tailwind for value/activist plays — just slow.
insights.quote: They're breaking up some of these cross shareholdings. They're... penalizing companies that trade at discounts to book value.

insights.claim: Loeb helped seed Japan's corporate-governance reform by co-writing an AEI paper (with Larry Lindsey and Niall Ferguson) urging Abe's 'three arrows' to add a fourth focus on corporate governance and return on invested capital, which was picked up by the WSJ and adopted.
insights.speaker: Dan Loeb
insights.so_what: Shows the policy origins of Japan's ROIC/governance push that now underpins the multi-year Japan re-rating trade.
insights.quote: my suggestion was that they needed to include corporate governance and in particular a focus on return on invested capital as part of their three arrow strategy

insights.claim: Loeb argues Middle East states — Bahrain, UAE, Saudi, Morocco, Azerbaijan — have become better US allies than NATO with far higher growth rates and faster technology adoption, and calls the region the most vibrant and interesting part of the world to invest.
insights.speaker: Dan Loeb
insights.so_what: Flags Gulf/MENA growth and tech adoption as an emerging allocation theme tied to geopolitical realignment.
insights.quote: who would have thought... Bahrain, the Emirates, Saudi, Morocco, Azar Bjan would be better allies to the US than NATO?

insights.claim: Loeb says the original Third Point Re thesis (2010 Bermuda reinsurer investing float in the hedge fund — a barbell with treasuries for tax deferral and leverage, modeled on Greenlight Re at 140% of book) failed because P&C reinsurance turned sharply for the worse; the fix was to pivot to plain-vanilla annuities.
insights.speaker: Dan Loeb
insights.so_what: Candid post-mortem on hedge-fund-reinsurer 'float' model; annuities + credit, not P&C + equity float, is the durable structure.
insights.quote: we had the right idea, but we had the... wrong insurance vehicle. We were doing PNC insurance. We really should have just done plain vanilla annuities

insights.claim: Third Point merged its reinsurer into the UK closed-end fund Third Point Offshore Investors, redomiciled it Guernsey-to-Cayman, and repurposed it from a hedge-fund feeder into an insurance company that will write reinsurance and primary annuities, with the money managed in private/structured credit, whole-loan mortgages, real-estate direct lending and IG corporates.
insights.speaker: Dan Loeb
insights.so_what: Maps Third Point's Apollo/Athene-style permanent-capital build; annuity liabilities feed a credit-heavy asset engine plus equity in junior structured tranches and growth equity.
insights.quote: we repurposed it from a closed end investor in my hedge fund into an insurance company... third point manages the money in private credit, structured credit, whole loan mortgages

themes.theme: Mistakes, AI-short risk, and the modern analyst

insights.claim: Loeb calls FTX his hardest lesson — it looked great and blockchain-verifiable with good co-investors, but wasn't what it seemed; Third Point now checks bank balances as basic diligence, and Loeb notes SBF's venture picks (Cursor, Anthropic, Solana) showed a genuine nose for value.
insights.speaker: Dan Loeb
insights.so_what: Diligence lesson — verify cash/bank balances on private deals; also a backhanded signal on the quality of the FTX/Alameda venture book.
insights.quote: I will say that now our due diligence process, we definitely like check bank balances and do like the most basic due diligence

insights.claim: Loeb says Third Point's recent mistakes came from being too confident that AI wouldn't disrupt certain info-services businesses or that incumbents' proprietary data was protected, even as it made good short bets on other AI-disrupted names; he expects an eventual shakeout with some 'phoenixes' rising.
insights.speaker: Dan Loeb
insights.so_what: Warning that 'proprietary data moat' is a weaker defense against AI than assumed; info-services incumbents are vulnerable longs and crowded-short candidates.
insights.quote: we thought we knew better that AI wasn't really going to... affect this part of the infoservices business or these guys had... proprietary information

insights.claim: Loeb says the great analyst today is a 'junior Gavin Baker' who deeply understands an industry and the nuances of a technology — not the 1990s archetype who could fast-build models and decode complex restructurings (as he once did with the Drexel Burnham bankruptcy claims).
insights.speaker: Dan Loeb
insights.so_what: Hiring/skill thesis: domain and technology depth beats financial-engineering speed in the AI era — relevant for talent and research-edge assessment.
insights.quote: now it's somebody who's like a Gavin Baker type, a junior Gavin Baker, somebody who understands a company or understands an industry and understands the nuances of a technology

insights.claim: Loeb cites Casey's General Stores as a top performer that looked like a tech stock because it was really a pizza chain masquerading as a convenience-store chain — surfaced by an analyst who went to Texas and ate the pizza, exemplifying gumshoe insight over data.
insights.speaker: Dan Loeb
insights.so_what: Reframes a 'boring' retailer as a high-margin food business — the kind of non-obvious reclassification that drives mispricing alpha.
insights.quote: they were not a convenience store chain... they were a pizza chain... masquerading as convenience stores

insights.claim: Loeb says Danaher was his most instructive investment — a ~4-year winner that taught him how the Danaher Business System (Kaizen continuous improvement, accountability where underperformance is 'celebrated' because fixable, shedding low-quality units for higher-ROIC ones) creates durable quality; he sold it but recently re-entered small after the selloff.
insights.speaker: Dan Loeb
insights.so_what: A template for spotting operating-system-driven compounders; the recent small re-entry signals renewed (cautious) interest after weakness.
insights.quote: what they have is a whole system of implementing improvement across the organization

themes.theme: AI as a research tool & firm process

insights.claim: Loeb is mandating firm-wide AI adoption — hiring native computer scientists and a system integrator to coach the team, with some analysts running agents overnight burning huge token volumes — framing Claude as a tool that makes individuals autonomous self-improvers who get back proportional to what they put in.
insights.speaker: Dan Loeb
insights.so_what: A bellwether hedge fund operationalizing AI in research workflow; signals rising buy-side AI/token spend and a coaching/integration org model.
insights.quote: some people are running agents overnight and using tons and tons of tokens. Other people are... probably more like me, just using it more for queries

insights.claim: Loeb positions Third Point as more optimistic / less doom-prone than peers on AI, expecting it to create net jobs and opportunities, and says the firm's true differentiator is the ability to default into credit investing during stressed times — a comfort few equity-rooted peers share.
insights.speaker: Dan Loeb
insights.so_what: The stated edge is optionality — pivot to credit in a real credit cycle; relevant if a long-absent (post-2020) credit cycle finally arrives.
insights.quote: the differentiator for us is that we can always... default into credit investing... I'm very comfortable investing in... incredibly stressed times

insights.claim: Loeb says credit can't be done by tourists — it requires standing relationships across the ~$1.5T high-yield, ~$1.5T broadly-syndicated-loan and ~$6T structured-credit markets — which is why Third Point built out CLO and structured-credit franchises rather than dabbling when opportunities arise.
insights.speaker: Dan Loeb
insights.so_what: Sizes the credit opportunity set and explains the relationship moat; you must be permanently wired in to capture dislocations.
insights.quote: these are not markets that lend themselves to... tourism when the opportunities really come up. We're already there with the relationships
